senses.definition: A peptide hormone made in the pancreas that is released with insulin and helps control blood sugar levels.
examples: Amylin is secreted by the same cells in the pancreas that produce insulin.
Researchers are studying how amylin affects appetite and blood glucose control.
A synthetic form of amylin is sometimes used to help treat diabetes.

senses.definition: Used in scientific words to mean starch or a starch-like substance.
examples: In medical terms, the prefix amylo- often refers to starch or starch-like deposits.
Words like amyloidosis and amylopectin both start with the combining form amylo-.
The element amylo- comes from a Greek word for starch.

senses.definition: A plant carbohydrate that contains both starch-like and cellulose parts in the same material.
examples: Amylocellulose occurs in some plant tissues as a mixture of starchy and fibrous components.
The chemist isolated amylocellulose to study how it breaks down in the gut.
Amylocellulose links properties of starch and cellulose in one compound.

senses.definition: Breaking down or destroying starch.
examples: Amyloclastic enzymes are responsible for splitting starch into smaller sugars.
The amyloclastic action of the bacteria led to a fall in starch content.
An amyloclastic process converts complex starch molecules into dextrins.

senses.definition: An enzyme that causes starch to thicken or clot.
examples: Amylocoagulase was added to the solution to observe its effect on starch gels.
The researcher measured how quickly amylocoagulase changed the texture of the starch mixture.
Amylocoagulase activity depends on temperature and pH.

senses.definition: A soluble dextrin formed when starch is partly broken down.
examples: Amylodextrin is produced during the hydrolysis of starch into sugar.
Soluble amylodextrin appears at an early stage of starch digestion.
The chemist analyzed amylodextrin as an intermediate product of starch breakdown.

senses.definition: Difficulty or inability to digest starchy foods.
examples: Patients with amylodyspepsia may feel discomfort after eating bread or potatoes.
The doctor diagnosed amylodyspepsia based on the patient’s response to starchy meals.
Dietary changes can help manage symptoms of amylodyspepsia.

senses.definition: The water-soluble part of starch granules.
examples: Amylogen can be separated from the insoluble part of starch by careful washing.
The soluble amylogen fraction is important in some food processes.
Scientists examined amylogen to understand how starch behaves in water.

senses.definition: The formation of starch in plants or tissues.
examples: Amylogenesis occurs in plant cells as they store energy in the form of starch.
The study focused on factors that influence amylogenesis in developing seeds.
Changes in temperature can affect amylogenesis in some crops.

senses.definition: Producing or forming starch.
examples: Amylogenic tissues actively convert sugars into stored starch.
The researcher identified an amylogenic zone in the root.
Amylogenic activity increased during the later stages of development.

senses.definition: Plural of amylogen, referring to water-soluble parts of starch granules.
examples: Different amylogens may have slightly different properties in solution.
The mixture contained several amylogens derived from cereal starch.
Researchers compared amylogens from various plant sources.

senses.definition: The chemical breakdown of starch by adding water, often helped by enzymes.
examples: Amylohydrolysis converts starch into smaller sugars like dextrins and maltose.
Industrial processes use controlled amylohydrolysis to make syrups from starch.
The rate of amylohydrolysis depends on temperature and enzyme concentration.

senses.definition: Breaking down starch by hydrolysis.
examples: Amylohydrolytic enzymes play a key role in digestion.
The amylohydrolytic reaction released simple sugars from the starch granules.
They compared the amylohydrolytic effects of different enzyme preparations.

senses.definition: An abnormal protein that can build up in organs and tissues, often causing disease.
examples: Amyloid deposits were found in the patient’s heart tissue.
Alzheimer’s disease is associated with amyloid plaques in the brain.
The test detects amyloid in blood and other body fluids.

senses.definition: Relating to or containing amyloid.
examples: The biopsy showed amyloid material in the kidney.
They studied the structure of amyloid fibrils under the microscope.
Amyloid changes were present in several organs.

senses.definition: Having the nature of or resembling amyloid.
examples: The pathologist described the tissue as amyloidal in appearance.
Amyloidal deposits may interfere with normal organ function.
They observed amyloidal material in the blood vessel walls.

senses.definition: Plural of amyloidosis, referring to several diseases caused by amyloid deposits in organs or tissues.
examples: Different amyloidoses are classified by the type of amyloid protein involved.
Systemic amyloidoses can affect multiple organs at the same time.
Researchers are developing new treatments for hereditary amyloidoses.

senses.definition: A disease in which abnormal amyloid protein builds up in organs or tissues and interferes with their normal work.
examples: The patient was diagnosed with cardiac amyloidosis after several tests.
Amyloidosis can cause kidney failure if it is not treated.
Symptoms of amyloidosis depend on which organs are affected.

senses.definition: A less common plural form of amyloidosis, meaning several types or cases of that disease.
examples: Various amyloidosises may present with similar symptoms.
Genetic factors play a role in some amyloidosises.
Clinicians must distinguish among different amyloidosises for proper treatment.

senses.definition: Plural of amyloid, meaning abnormal proteins that can build up in organs and tissues.
examples: Different amyloids are linked to different diseases.
Scientists study how amyloids form and spread in the body.
The brain tissue contained amyloids typical of Alzheimer’s disease.

senses.definition: A variety of the mineral leucite that has a starch-like reaction with iodine.
examples: Amyloleucite shows a blue color with iodine, similar to starch.
The geologist identified the sample as amyloleucite based on its optical properties.
Amyloleucite is a rare mineral described in older mineralogy texts.

senses.definition: Plural of amylolysis, meaning several instances or types of starch breakdown.
examples: Different amylolyses occur under acid and enzymatic conditions.
The study compared amylolyses in various cereal flours.
Industrial processes involve controlled amylolyses to produce sweeteners.

senses.definition: The chemical breakdown of starch into smaller sugars, usually by enzymes or acids.
examples: Amylolysis is an important step in the digestion of carbohydrates.
The brewer controls amylolysis to get the right sugar profile in the wort.
They measured the products of amylolysis during the reaction.

senses.definition: Breaking down starch into simpler sugars.
examples: Amylolytic enzymes such as amylases are active in saliva and the small intestine.
They compared the amylolytic activity of different bacterial strains.
The fungus showed strong amylolytic properties.

senses.definition: A rare or obsolete term for a tumor or mass made up of amyloid material.
examples: Older medical texts sometimes refer to an amyloid tumor as an amylom.
The surgeon removed an amylom from the patient’s liver.
The word amylom is largely historical in modern medicine.

senses.definition: A variant or misspelling of amylom; an amyloid tumor or mass.
examples: The case report mentioned an amylome in the spleen.
Amylome is an uncommon term in present-day pathology.
The surgeon removed the amylome and sent it for analysis.

senses.definition: An instrument used to measure how much starch is in a sample.
examples: The technician used an amylometer to test the starch content of the flour.
The amylometer reading showed a high level of starch in the solution.
Early food laboratories often relied on the amylometer for quality control.

senses.definition: An older term for starch or a starch-like substance.
examples: Some nineteenth-century texts refer to starch as amylon.
The chemist experimented with purified amylon from potatoes.
The word amylon is rarely used in modern scientific writing.

senses.definition: The branched part of starch made of many glucose units linked in a tree-like structure.
examples: Amylopectin makes up most of the starch in waxy maize.
Because of its branching, amylopectin is digested more quickly than amylose.
The ratio of amylose to amylopectin affects the texture of cooked rice.

senses.definition: Plural of amylopectin, referring to several types or sources of branched starch molecules.
examples: Different plant amylopectins show different branching patterns.
The researchers compared cereal amylopectins with those from tubers.
These two amylopectins behaved differently during heating.

senses.definition: A condition in which a person has a strong, often abnormal desire to eat starch, such as raw flour or laundry starch.
examples: Amylophagia is sometimes seen in pregnancy or iron-deficiency anemia.
The patient’s amylophagia led her to eat large amounts of cornstarch.
Doctors may treat amylophagia by addressing underlying nutritional problems.

senses.definition: A compound in which a starch molecule or part of it is joined to a phosphate group.
examples: The presence of amylophosphate can change the properties of starch gels.
Enzymes may form amylophosphate as an intermediate in starch metabolism.
The chemist prepared an amylophosphate derivative for further study.

senses.definition: Relating to or containing an amylophosphate or a starch-phosphate group.
examples: They analyzed the amylophosphoric compounds formed during the reaction.
The amylophosphoric bond plays a role in energy transfer in some pathways.
Amylophosphoric esters are of interest in carbohydrate chemistry.

senses.definition: A type of plastid in plant cells that stores starch.
examples: Amyloplasts are especially common in potato tubers and seeds.
Under the microscope, amyloplasts appear as bodies filled with starch grains.
The study examined how amyloplasts develop in root cells.

senses.definition: Relating to or forming amyloplasts, or forming starch in plastids.
examples: Amyloplastic tissue is responsible for starch accumulation in the storage organ.
The amyloplastic activity increased during seed maturation.
These cells show strong amyloplastic differentiation.

senses.definition: Another term for amyloplast, a starch-storing plastid in plant cells.
examples: The amyloplastid is filled with layered starch grains.
Root amyloplastids help the plant sense gravity.
The study described changes in amyloplastids during germination.

senses.definition: Plural of amyloplast, meaning starch-storing plastids in plant cells.
examples: Amyloplasts accumulate in storage organs like tubers and seeds.
The researchers observed amyloplasts moving within the cell.
Amyloplasts can change position in response to gravity.

senses.definition: An enzyme that breaks down starch, especially in plants or microorganisms.
examples: Amylopsase activity increased during germination.
The amylopsase converted starch into simpler sugars.
They purified amylopsase from a fungal culture.

senses.definition: A starch-digesting enzyme found in pancreatic juice.
examples: Amylopsin helps break down starch in the small intestine.
The pancreas secretes amylopsin along with other digestive enzymes.
Low levels of amylopsin can lead to poor carbohydrate digestion.

senses.definition: Plural of amylopsin, referring to starch-digesting enzymes from pancreatic juice in different species or forms.
examples: The study compared amylopsins from several mammalian species.
Different amylopsins show different optimal pH ranges.
The researchers purified several amylopsins for analysis.

senses.definition: The mainly straight-chain part of starch made of many glucose units.
examples: Rice with high amylose content tends to be less sticky when cooked.
Amylose forms helical structures that can trap iodine.
The balance between amylose and amylopectin affects the texture of starch gels.

senses.definition: Plural of amylose, referring to amylose molecules or types from different sources.
examples: The study compared amyloses extracted from wheat, maize, and potatoes.
These amyloses varied in chain length and branching.
High-amylose starches contain amyloses with longer chains.

senses.definition: The conversion or breakdown of starch, especially by enzymes.
examples: Amylosis in the digestive tract provides glucose for energy.
The experiment tracked the products formed during amylosis.
In brewing, controlled amylosis is essential for proper fermentation.

senses.definition: The process of building starch molecules from smaller sugar units.
examples: Enzymes involved in amylosynthesis link glucose units into long chains.
Amylosynthesis is active in plant storage tissues during growth.
The study examined the regulation of amylosynthesis in potato tubers.

senses.definition: Plural of amyl, a chemical group with five carbon atoms found in some organic compounds.
examples: The chemist compared different amyls used as solvents.
These amyls differ slightly in structure but have similar properties.
Some amyls are components of flavor and fragrance mixtures.

senses.definition: A Latin-based or older scientific word for starch.
examples: Pharmacists once used the term amylum for medicinal starch.
The label listed amylum as one of the tablet excipients.
In some languages, amylum is still used in technical contexts.

senses.definition: Plural of amylum, referring to several kinds or samples of starch.
examples: The pharmacist compared different amylums for use as tablet fillers.
These amylums varied in particle size and flow properties.
Older texts list various plant amylums used in pharmacy.

senses.definition: The presence of starch in the urine.
examples: Amyluria is rare because starch is usually digested before absorption.
The laboratory report mentioned mild amyluria in the patient.
Amyluria may indicate a problem with digestion or kidney function.

senses.definition: An extinct mammal related to rhinoceroses that lived in the Eocene and Oligocene periods.
examples: Fossils of Amynodon have been found in North America and Asia.
Amynodon had a heavy body and likely lived near water.
Paleontologists study Amynodon to understand early rhinoceros evolution.

senses.definition: A member of the extinct group of mammals related to Amynodon and rhinoceroses.
examples: The site contained bones of several amynodonts.
Amynodonts are thought to have been semi-aquatic animals.
The amynodont is an important fossil in rhinoceros evolution.

senses.definition: Weakness or loss of strength in the muscles.
examples: The patient complained of amyosthenia after a long illness.
Amyosthenia can be a symptom of several neuromuscular diseases.
Physical therapy may help relieve mild amyosthenia.
